entries.literal: to shape, create (used exclusively of divine activity)
entries.definition: To create; to bring into existence something new. In the Qal stem, God is always the subject.
entries.usage: Appears at the pivotal moments of Genesis 1 — the creation of the cosmos, of animal life, and of humanity.

entries.definition: The covenant name of God, revealed to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3). Rendered 'the LORD' in small capitals in most English translations.
entries.usage: Used over 6,800 times in the Old Testament, especially in covenant contexts.

entries.definition: To shepherd: to feed, guide, protect, and care for a flock. Used of literal shepherds and figuratively of God and of kings.
entries.usage: The word behind 'shepherd' in Psalm 23:1; also used of David's occupation before kingship.

entries.definition: Steadfast love, loving-kindness, mercy: love that keeps covenant faithfully regardless of circumstance. One of the richest words in the Hebrew Bible.
entries.usage: Rendered 'mercy' in Psalm 23:6 and 'lovingkindness' throughout the Psalms; central to God's self-description in Exodus 34:6.

entries.definition: To trust with a sense of well-founded security; to place full weight upon something confident it will hold.
entries.usage: The word behind 'trust' in Proverbs 3:5 — a bodily image of leaning one's whole weight on the LORD.

entries.definition: Peace understood as total well-being: health, prosperity, harmony with God and neighbor — far richer than absence of conflict.
entries.usage: The standard Hebrew greeting; the 'peace' Jeremiah promises in Jeremiah 29:11.

entries.definition: Word, message, reason. In John 1, the eternal Word — God's self-expression — identified as the pre-existent Christ.
entries.usage: John deliberately echoes Genesis 1 ('In the beginning') and engages Greek philosophy's use of logos for the rational principle of the cosmos.

entries.definition: To love with deliberate, self-giving commitment; love as an act of the will, not merely feeling.
entries.usage: The verb of John 3:16 — 'God so loved the world.'

entries.definition: Self-giving, unconditional love; the character of God himself (1 John 4:8). The KJV renders it 'charity' in 1 Corinthians 13.
entries.usage: Paul's subject in 1 Corinthians 13; the greatest of faith, hope, and love.

entries.definition: Trusting confidence in God and his promises; both the act of believing and the content believed.
entries.usage: Defined in Hebrews 11:1 and illustrated by the whole 'hall of faith' that follows.

entries.definition: Substance, assurance, confident foundation. In commercial papyri it meant a title-deed — documented guarantee of ownership.
entries.usage: 'Faith is the substance (hypostasis) of things hoped for' — Hebrews 11:1.

entries.definition: Peace: tranquility of soul and right relationship with God, carrying the full weight of Hebrew shalom into Greek.
entries.usage: The 'peace of God which passeth all understanding' (Philippians 4:7) and the peace Jesus bequeaths in John 14:27.

entries.definition: To be anxious, to worry — from a root suggesting a mind divided and distracted.
entries.usage: Jesus' word in Matthew 6:25 ('take no thought') and Paul's in Philippians 4:6 ('be careful for nothing').

entries.definition: Only, unique, one-and-only. Describes Jesus' unique relationship to the Father.
entries.usage: 'Only begotten Son' in John 3:16; 'one and only Son' in modern translations.
